title: Vanilla marshmallows
subtitle: Gelatine-set, beaten-sugar marshmallows dusted with icing sugar and cornflour
excerpt: Twenty-four homemade vanilla marshmallows: gelatine dissolved in water, a sugar syrup cooked to 130°C and poured over the gelatine, then beaten until thick, white, and airy, poured into a dusted tin, and left to set overnight. Cut into cubes and rolled in icing sugar and cornflour.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: the pâte de guimauve section documents a confection of gelatine or gum arabic dissolved in water, a boiled sugar syrup poured over and beaten until white and spongy, then moulded and dusted with icing sugar. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg: Acton's sugar confection chapter documents the technique of beating a boiled syrup with gelatine to produce an aerated, spongy consistency. The marshmallow in its modern gelatine-and-beaten-sugar form is documented in British domestic confectionery from the Victorian period.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Marshmallows keep in an airtight tin at room temperature for up to 2 weeks. Do not refrigerate — they absorb moisture.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: target: 130°C for the sugar syrup — use a sugar thermometer

recipeTools.notes: Essential — both hands are needed to pour the hot syrup. A hand mixer propped in a bowl does not work safely.
20 × 20 cm, greased and dusted with the icing sugar and cornflour mixture.

glossaryTerms.definition: Soaking gelatine leaves (or powder) in cold water for 5 minutes before use. Dried gelatine is coiled protein strands that are too rigid to dissolve evenly in a hot liquid. Soaking in cold water (blooming) softens the strands and allows them to hydrate. Once bloomed, the gelatine is squeezed dry of excess water and dissolved in the warm liquid. Skipping the bloom step produces gelatine clumps that do not dissolve evenly, leaving lumps in the finished marshmallow.

content.text: Homemade marshmallows are made in the same spirit as Italian meringue: a hot sugar syrup poured slowly into a whipping mixture while it beats. Here the whipping mixture is bloomed gelatine rather than egg white. The
step is not optional — it determines whether the gelatine dissolves evenly into the syrup or forms clumps. A stand mixer is required: both hands must be free to manage the hot syrup pour.

attrs.title: Hot sugar is extremely dangerous
attrs.body: The sugar syrup reaches 130°C. Pour it slowly down the inside of the mixer bowl, avoiding the whisk and your hands. Keep children well away. If any syrup contacts skin, run immediately under cold water for at least 10 minutes.

items.prepNote: for dusting the tin and rolling the cut marshmallows
mixed with the icing sugar — prevents sticking

content.text: Bloom the gelatine and prepare the tin

Soak the {{gelatine-leaves}} gelatine leaves in the {{water}} cold water for 5 minutes until soft. Mix the {{icing-sugar}} and {{cornflour}} together. Dust a 20 × 20 cm tin generously all over with the icing sugar and cornflour mixture — base and sides.
Dissolve gelatine and make the syrup

Squeeze the bloomed {{gelatine-leaves}} gelatine leaves dry of excess water and place in the bowl of a stand mixer. Combine the {{caster-sugar}}, {{golden-syrup}}, and remaining {{water}} in a saucepan over medium heat. Stir until the sugar dissolves, then stop stirring and bring to a boil. Cook until the temperature on a sugar thermometer reads 130°C. Pour the hot syrup slowly and carefully in a thin stream down the inside of the mixer bowl , keeping well away from the whisk, onto the gelatine, with the mixer running on low. The heat of the syrup will melt the gelatine.

Once all the syrup is in, increase the speed to high and beat for 8–10 minutes until the mixture is thick, very white, glossy, and holds soft peaks. Add {{vanilla-extract}} in the last minute of beating.

Working quickly, pour the marshmallow mixture into the dusted tin. Smooth the top with a wet palette knife or wet fingers. Dust the surface generously with the icing sugar and cornflour mixture. Leave uncovered at room temperature to set for at least 8 hours, or overnight. Cut into 24 squares with a sharp, wet knife, dipping the blade in hot water between cuts. Roll each marshmallow in the remaining dusting mixture to coat all sides. Store in an airtight tin.
